19: to soma hymon naos tou en hymin hagiou pneumatos estin (to sōma hymōn naos tou en hymin hagiou pneumatos estin): 'your body is a temple of the Holy Spirit within you.' Here the body (sōma) is singular — each believer's individual body is a sanctuary of the Spirit. This shifts 1 Corinthians 3:16's corporate sanctuary-language to the individual person. The implication for sexual ethics (1 Corinthians 6:15-18: not prostitution) is that bodily sin is sanctuary-desecration — the fornication charge is framed not as moral failure but as spatial-theological violation of the Spirit's dwelling place. Agorazō (to purchase, 1 Corinthians 6:20) is commercial-legal redemption language: you were bought at a price, therefore you are not your own property to dispose of as you wish.
1: tolmai (dare) — the verb implies moral effrontery, not merely courage. The act requires a kind of audacity. epi tōn adikōn (before the unrighteous) — adikoi here is not a generic moral epithet but a technical category: those outside the covenant community, who by definition lack the standard of righteousness that would qualify them to adjudicate covenant disputes. epi tōn hagiōn (before the saints) — the church as the community of the holy, the proper forum for internal disputes.
2: ouk oidate hoti hoi hagioi ton kosmon krienousi (do you not know that the saints will judge the world?) — the ouk oidate (do you not know) formula recurs eight times in 1 Corinthians (1 Corinthians 6:2, 3, 9, 15, 16, 19; 1 Corinthians 5:6; 1 Corinthians 3:16) — Paul appeals to a body of shared knowledge the Corinthians already possess but are not applying. The saints judging the world is a Second Temple expectation (Daniel 7:22; Wis 1 Corinthians 3:7-8; 1 En. 1 Corinthians 1:9): the redeemed community participates in eschatological judgment. If they will judge the cosmos, they are surely competent for petty disputes (kritēria elachistōn, smallest cases).
3: angelous krienoumen (we will judge angels) — not a speculative assertion but a shared eschatological belief. Paul assumes his readers accept this; he appeals to it to shame their failure at the lower task of judging this-life matters (biōtika, things of this life).
4: exouthenēmenous en tē ekklēsia (those who are held in contempt in the church) — either ironic (Paul is sarcastically suggesting they are appointing the scorned as judges) or a genuine re-evaluation: those the world scorns are qualified by their standing in the community. The sentence is interrogative or imperatival — scholars debate whether Paul is mocking their behavior or directing it. Either reading surfaces the absurdity of taking Christian disputes before pagan courts.
5: pros entropēn (to shame you) — Paul invokes the honor-shame framework directly. sophos (wise person): the vocabulary of wisdom picks up Paul's sustained argument from 1 Corinthians 1–4. The Corinthians pride themselves on wisdom (sophia); Paul now says their legal behavior proves there is not one person wise enough to resolve a dispute between siblings in Christ. This is a deliberate inversion of their self-image.
6: adelphos meta adelphou krinetai (brother goes to court against brother) — the repetition of adelphos (brother) intensifies the contradiction: the covenant-bond of siblinghood is violated by the very act of external litigation. kai touto epi apistoōn (and this before unbelievers) — the public visibility makes it a witness-failure, not only a community-failure.
7: hēttēma (defeat, loss): a legal-commercial term; already, before the verdict is rendered, the very existence of the lawsuit is a defeat. The Stoic/Cynic philosophical tradition valued being wronged without retaliation as the mark of the sage; Paul invokes this expectation — ouchi mallon adikeisthe? ouchi mallon apostereisthe? (why not rather be wronged? why not rather be cheated?) — but grounds it not in philosophical virtue but in community honor and eschatological perspective.
8: alla hymeis adikeite kai apostereite (instead, you yourselves do wrong and cheat) — the reversal: the Corinthians are not merely failing to endure wrong; they are actively perpetrating it against brothers and sisters. Apostereō (defraud) is the same verb used in 7:5 for depriving a spouse — here it is economic exploitation within the community.
9: adikoi (the unrighteous) connects to 1 Corinthians 6:1's epi tōn adikōn: Paul now defines the category from the inside. The vice catalogue that follows is one of Paul's four (others: Romans 1:29-31; Galatians 5:19-21; Colossians 3:5-8). malakoi (malakoi, lit. soft ones): used in secular Greek for the passive partner in male same-sex acts; also for men who adopted feminine roles. arsenokoitai (arsenokoitai): a Pauline coinage (earliest known use); formed from arsēn (male) + koitē (bed/sexual intercourse) — the compound reflects the LXX of Leviticus 18:22 (meta arsenos koitēn gynaikos) and 1 Corinthians 20:13 (hos an koimēthē meta arsenos koitēn gynaikos). Paul constructed the term from the Torah text that prohibits the act; it denotes the active male partner.
10: The vice list concludes with kleptai (thieves), pleonektai (greedy/covetous), methysoi (drunkards), loidoroi (slanderers), harpages (swindlers). None of these, Paul says, will inherit the kingdom (basileian Theou ou klēronomēsousin). The list is not hierarchical — no single vice is worse than others. All represent modes of living oriented away from God and toward self-assertion.
11: kai tauta tines ēte (and some of you were these things) — perfect past: the list of 1 Corinthians 6:9-10 described some of the Corinthians' pre-conversion lives. The aorist passive triad that follows is the hinge of the sentence: apelousasthe (you were washed — baptism), hēgiasthēte (you were sanctified — set apart/made holy), edikaiōthēte (you were justified — declared righteous before God). All three are aorist passives: God's action, not the believer's. The agency is double: en tō onomati tou Kyriou Iēsou Christou (in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ) and en tō pneumati tou Theou hēmōn (by the Spirit of our God). Identity transformation through divine action is the ground for the ethical demand.
12: panta moi exestin (I have the right to do anything) — twice quoted, twice qualified. This is a Corinthian slogan, not Paul's own assertion. The community apparently argued that Christian freedom (grace, not law) entails no moral constraints. Paul does not deny the slogan but applies two limits: all ou panta sympherei (not everything is beneficial/profitable) — the communal frame; and all ouk egō exousiasthēsomai hypo tinos (I will not be mastered by anything) — the personal frame. The word exousia (authority/right) recurs in different grammatical forms: freedom is not the abolition of authority but its proper exercise.
13: ta brōmata tē koilia kai hē koilia tois brōmasin (food for the stomach and the stomach for food) — another Corinthian slogan Paul quotes before demolishing. The argument: just as digestion is morally neutral, so sex is morally neutral. Paul's counter is christological: the body is ou tē porneia alla tō Kyriō (not for sexual immorality but for the Lord), and the Lord is for the body. This moves the body out of the category of morally neutral biological function and into the category of Christ's own — a property relationship that reframes all bodily ethics.
14: ho de Theos kai ton Kyrion ēgeiren kai hēmas exegerei (by his power God raised the Lord from the dead, and he will raise us also) — the resurrection of Christ establishes the body's eschatological dignity. If the body participates in resurrection, it cannot be treated as a morally neutral organism. The future exegerei (will raise) is the ground of the present ethics: the body that will be raised is the body that is now joined to Christ.
15: ouk oidate hoti ta sōmata hymōn melē Christou estin (do you not know that your bodies are members of Christ?) — melē (members, limbs) is the body-of-Christ vocabulary: the individual body is a member of Christ's own body. aras oun ta melē tou Christou poiēsō pornēs melē (shall I then take the members of Christ and make them members of a prostitute?) — the question is rhetorical and its answer (mē genoito, never!) is Paul's strongest negative formula, used only when the option is theologically impossible.
16: kollōmenos tē pornē hen sōma estin (the one who unites himself with a prostitute is one body with her) — Paul applies the one-flesh principle from Genesis 2:24 (esontai hoi dyo eis sarka mian) to the prostitute encounter. The sexual union creates a real bodily bond (hen sōma), not a merely physical exchange. The argument depends on the body's non-neutrality: sex does something metaphysically, not just biologically.
17: ho de kollōmenos tō Kyriō hen pneuma estin (but the one who is united to the Lord is one spirit with him) — the parallelism with 1 Corinthians 6:16 is deliberate. Both relationships are characterized by union (kollaō, cleave/join); both produce a oneness; but the union with the Lord is pneumatic (spiritual), not merely somatic. This does not spiritualize the body away — the body is the site of both unions — but establishes the qualitative difference between the two allegiances.
18: pheugete tēn porneian (flee from sexual immorality) — aorist imperative of flight, the same word used of Joseph fleeing Potiphar's wife (LXX Genesis 39:12). The call to flee rather than resist is significant: porneia is not an enemy to engage but a snare to escape. pan hamartēma ho ean poiēsē anthrōpos ektos tou sōmatos estin — every sin a person commits is outside the body; but sexual immorality is eis to idion sōma (against one's own body). The precise meaning of this uniqueness is debated, but Paul's point is that bodily sexual sin constitutes a distinctive violation of the body's integrity as the locus of Christ-union and Spirit-indwelling.
20: ēgorasthēte gar timēs (you were bought at a price) — the commercial-legal metaphor: the believer has been purchased from their prior owner (sin, death, the powers). Agorazō is the marketplace verb for buying; used of slave redemption. The implication is a transfer of ownership: they are not their own (ouk este heautōn). doxasate dē ton Theon en tō sōmati hymōn (therefore glorify God with your body) — the imperativis grounded in the indicative: because you were purchased, honor God with the body that belongs to him. The body is the site of glorification, not an obstacle to it.
